1	# Prime Research: Computing p(n) Exactly Without Bruteforcing
2	
3	## Project Goal
4	Find an efficient algorithm to compute the nth prime number p(n) without
5	enumeration, sieving, or brute force. Target: p(10^100) in <1 second, 100% exact.
6	
7	## Status (April 2026)
8	- **390+ approaches tested** across 11 sessions, 93+ sub-agents
9	- **ALL KNOWN PATHS CLOSED** but no proof that polylog is impossible
10	- **Problem is GENUINELY OPEN** -- no unconditional lower bound beyond Omega(log x)
11	- Best exact: `algorithms/v10_c_accelerated.py` -- O(p(n)^{2/3}), p(10^9) in 0.175s
12	- Best approximate: R^{-1}(n) -- O(polylog), ~47% digits correct
13	
14	## Directory Layout
15	
16	```
17	status/
18	  CLOSED_PATHS.md    <-- SEARCH HERE before proposing ANY approach (380+ entries)
19	  OPEN_PROBLEMS.md   <-- The ONLY viable research directions
20	  BEST_ALGORITHMS.md <-- Working implementations with benchmarks
21	proven/
22	  barriers.md        <-- Mathematically proven impossibility results
23	  complexity.md      <-- Upper/lower bounds, circuit complexity
24	  information.md     <-- Entropy, Kolmogorov complexity, entanglement
25	  quantum.md         <-- Why quantum computing doesn't help
26	novel/
27	  info_computation_gap.md  <-- Best original insight (delta(n) framing)
28	  entropy_measurements.md  <-- Empirical: 5.04 bits/prime, gap analysis
29	  failure_taxonomy.md      <-- Three failure modes classification
30	algorithms/              <-- ONLY working, tested code
31	literature/
32	  references.md          <-- All citations in one place
33	  state_of_art_2026.md   <-- Latest published results
34	experiments/             <-- ALL experiments organized by topic
35	  analytic/ algebraic/ quantum/ ml/ information_theory/
36	  dynamical/ topological/ sieve/ other/
37	data/                    <-- Zeta zeros (200/300/500/1000) for explicit formula work
38	archive/                 <-- Session logs and visualizations
39	```
40	
41	## The Barrier in One Paragraph
42	p(n) = SMOOTH(n) + RANDOM(n). The smooth part R^{-1}(n) is O(polylog) and gives
43	~50% of digits. The remaining ~50% encode oscillatory contributions of ~10^48
44	Riemann zeta zeros with GUE-random phases, information-theoretically incompressible.
45	Best known: O(x^{2/3}) combinatorial, O(x^{1/2+epsilon}) analytic. For p(10^100):
46	minimum ~10^49 operations. At 10^15 ops/sec = 10^34 seconds = 10^26 years.
47	
48	## Three Failure Modes (Every Failed Approach Falls Into One)
49	1. **Circularity** -- needs primes to compute primes (Mills, Chebotarev, EGF)
50	2. **Equivalence** -- reduces to explicit formula / zeta zero sum (all spectral methods)
51	3. **Information Loss** -- smooth approximations lose ~170 bits distinguishing p(n)
52	
53	## Do NOT Re-explore These (Thoroughly Closed)
54	- Convergence acceleration on explicit formula (Richardson/Levin/Weniger all fail; error oscillatory not smooth -- Session 11)
55	- Alternative decompositions of pi(x) (Buchstab/Vaughan/hyperbola/FFT all O(x^{2/3}) -- Session 11)
56	- TC^0 direct paths (Legendre/Lucy DP/Möbius/partial sieve all fail -- Session 11)
57	- Zeta zero summation (always O(sqrt(x)) terms, GUE prevents compression)
58	- ML/neural (1.1% best, fundamentally limited by Prime Coding Theorem)
59	- Modular/CRT reconstruction (circular or same complexity as pi(x))
60	- Quantum algorithms (volume-law entanglement, no speedup beyond O(x^{1/3}))
61	- Exotic algebra (topos/motivic/prismatic/condensed/perfectoid -- all closed)
62	- Dynamical systems (FRACTRAN/CA/transfer operators -- all closed)
63	- p-adic/adelic (not q-adically continuous, Mahler coefficients diverge)
64	- Interpolation/regression on delta(n) (random walk, zero generalization)
65	
66	## Viable Research Directions
67	1. **Circuit complexity of pi(x)** -- TC^0? NC^1? KEY RESULT (Session 11):
68	   AKS path to TC^0 requires GROWING-dim matrix powering (k=polylog(n)).
69	   Fixed-k MPOW IS in TC^0 (RAIRO 2000), but growing-k needs depth O(log log n).
70	   IMP_k (k≥3, different matrices) NOT in TC^0 unless TC^0=NC^1.
71	   Growing-dim MPOW sits precisely at the TC^0/NC^1 frontier -- genuinely open.
72	   Also: Healy-Viola 2006 shows F_{2^n} exponentiation IS in TC^0 (Frobenius),
73	   but doesn't help over Z_n. Key open: can commutative ring structure help?
74	2. **Time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity of delta(n)** (open, connects to circuit bounds)
75	3. **Zeta zero compressibility** -- convergence acceleration CLOSED (Session 11);
76	   only STRUCTURAL approaches remain (find global pattern in zero distribution)
77	4. **Concrete Berry-Keating Hamiltonian** -- Hilbert-Polya conjecture (quantum, open)
78	5. **Novel number-theoretic identity** relating sum_rho R(x^rho) to computable function
79	6. **Combinatorial pi(x) in O(x^{3/5})?** -- H-T achieved this for M(x) in 2021,
80	   but pi(x) lacks signed cancellation. Open whether transfer is possible.
81	
82	## Rules for AI Agents
83	1. **Read `status/CLOSED_PATHS.md` before proposing ANY approach**
84	2. Save experiments to `experiments/<topic>/` with descriptive filenames
85	3. Update `status/CLOSED_PATHS.md` when closing an approach (add a row)
86	4. Genuinely novel findings go to `novel/` with evidence
87	5. New literature goes to `literature/`
88	6. **UPDATE this CLAUDE.md** when you discover something that changes the picture:
89	   - New barrier proven? Update "The Barrier" and "Do NOT Re-explore" sections
90	   - New viable direction found? Add to "Viable Research Directions"
91	   - Closed a direction from OPEN_PROBLEMS.md? Move it to "Do NOT Re-explore"
92	   - Found a better algorithm? Update "Status" section and `status/BEST_ALGORITHMS.md`
93	   - Keep this file accurate and current -- future sessions depend on it
94	7. If you beat the current best algorithm, save it to `algorithms/` with benchmarks
95	8. DO NOT modify `run.sh`
96	9. Use sub-agents to save context window
97	10. When you run out of context, just stop -- the system will restart you
98	11. If you find the breakthrough, respond with exactly: I FOUND IT!!!
99

# Prime Research: Computing p(n) Exactly Without Bruteforcing

## Project Goal
Find an efficient algorithm to compute the nth prime number p(n) without
enumeration, sieving, or brute force. Target: p(10^100) in <1 second, 100% exact.

## Status (April 2026)
- **390+ approaches tested** across 11 sessions, 93+ sub-agents
- **ALL KNOWN PATHS CLOSED** but no proof that polylog is impossible
- **Problem is GENUINELY OPEN** -- no unconditional lower bound beyond Omega(log x)
- Best exact: `algorithms/v10_c_accelerated.py` -- O(p(n)^{2/3}), p(10^9) in 0.175s
- Best approximate: R^{-1}(n) -- O(polylog), ~47% digits correct

## Directory Layout

```
status/
  CLOSED_PATHS.md    <-- SEARCH HERE before proposing ANY approach (380+ entries)
  OPEN_PROBLEMS.md   <-- The ONLY viable research directions
  BEST_ALGORITHMS.md <-- Working implementations with benchmarks
proven/
  barriers.md        <-- Mathematically proven impossibility results
  complexity.md      <-- Upper/lower bounds, circuit complexity
  information.md     <-- Entropy, Kolmogorov complexity, entanglement
  quantum.md         <-- Why quantum computing doesn't help
novel/
  info_computation_gap.md  <-- Best original insight (delta(n) framing)
  entropy_measurements.md  <-- Empirical: 5.04 bits/prime, gap analysis
  failure_taxonomy.md      <-- Three failure modes classification
algorithms/              <-- ONLY working, tested code
literature/
  references.md          <-- All citations in one place
  state_of_art_2026.md   <-- Latest published results
experiments/             <-- ALL experiments organized by topic
  analytic/ algebraic/ quantum/ ml/ information_theory/
  dynamical/ topological/ sieve/ other/
data/                    <-- Zeta zeros (200/300/500/1000) for explicit formula work
archive/                 <-- Session logs and visualizations
```

## The Barrier in One Paragraph
p(n) = SMOOTH(n) + RANDOM(n). The smooth part R^{-1}(n) is O(polylog) and gives
~50% of digits. The remaining ~50% encode oscillatory contributions of ~10^48
Riemann zeta zeros with GUE-random phases, information-theoretically incompressible.
Best known: O(x^{2/3}) combinatorial, O(x^{1/2+epsilon}) analytic. For p(10^100):
minimum ~10^49 operations. At 10^15 ops/sec = 10^34 seconds = 10^26 years.

## Three Failure Modes (Every Failed Approach Falls Into One)
1. **Circularity** -- needs primes to compute primes (Mills, Chebotarev, EGF)
2. **Equivalence** -- reduces to explicit formula / zeta zero sum (all spectral methods)
3. **Information Loss** -- smooth approximations lose ~170 bits distinguishing p(n)

## Do NOT Re-explore These (Thoroughly Closed)
- Convergence acceleration on explicit formula (Richardson/Levin/Weniger all fail; error oscillatory not smooth -- Session 11)
- Alternative decompositions of pi(x) (Buchstab/Vaughan/hyperbola/FFT all O(x^{2/3}) -- Session 11)
- TC^0 direct paths (Legendre/Lucy DP/Möbius/partial sieve all fail -- Session 11)
- Zeta zero summation (always O(sqrt(x)) terms, GUE prevents compression)
- ML/neural (1.1% best, fundamentally limited by Prime Coding Theorem)
- Modular/CRT reconstruction (circular or same complexity as pi(x))
- Quantum algorithms (volume-law entanglement, no speedup beyond O(x^{1/3}))
- Exotic algebra (topos/motivic/prismatic/condensed/perfectoid -- all closed)
- Dynamical systems (FRACTRAN/CA/transfer operators -- all closed)
- p-adic/adelic (not q-adically continuous, Mahler coefficients diverge)
- Interpolation/regression on delta(n) (random walk, zero generalization)

## Viable Research Directions
1. **Circuit complexity of pi(x)** -- TC^0? NC^1? KEY RESULT (Session 11):
   AKS path to TC^0 requires GROWING-dim matrix powering (k=polylog(n)).
   Fixed-k MPOW IS in TC^0 (RAIRO 2000), but growing-k needs depth O(log log n).
   IMP_k (k≥3, different matrices) NOT in TC^0 unless TC^0=NC^1.
   Growing-dim MPOW sits precisely at the TC^0/NC^1 frontier -- genuinely open.
   Also: Healy-Viola 2006 shows F_{2^n} exponentiation IS in TC^0 (Frobenius),
   but doesn't help over Z_n. Key open: can commutative ring structure help?
2. **Time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity of delta(n)** (open, connects to circuit bounds)
3. **Zeta zero compressibility** -- convergence acceleration CLOSED (Session 11);
   only STRUCTURAL approaches remain (find global pattern in zero distribution)
4. **Concrete Berry-Keating Hamiltonian** -- Hilbert-Polya conjecture (quantum, open)
5. **Novel number-theoretic identity** relating sum_rho R(x^rho) to computable function
6. **Combinatorial pi(x) in O(x^{3/5})?** -- H-T achieved this for M(x) in 2021,
   but pi(x) lacks signed cancellation. Open whether transfer is possible.

## Rules for AI Agents
1. **Read `status/CLOSED_PATHS.md` before proposing ANY approach**
2. Save experiments to `experiments/<topic>/` with descriptive filenames
3. Update `status/CLOSED_PATHS.md` when closing an approach (add a row)
4. Genuinely novel findings go to `novel/` with evidence
5. New literature goes to `literature/`
6. **UPDATE this CLAUDE.md** when you discover something that changes the picture:
   - New barrier proven? Update "The Barrier" and "Do NOT Re-explore" sections
   - New viable direction found? Add to "Viable Research Directions"
   - Closed a direction from OPEN_PROBLEMS.md? Move it to "Do NOT Re-explore"
   - Found a better algorithm? Update "Status" section and `status/BEST_ALGORITHMS.md`
   - Keep this file accurate and current -- future sessions depend on it
7. If you beat the current best algorithm, save it to `algorithms/` with benchmarks
8. DO NOT modify `run.sh`
9. Use sub-agents to save context window
10. When you run out of context, just stop -- the system will restart you
11. If you find the breakthrough, respond with exactly: I FOUND IT!!!

content.content:
1	# Open Problems: Viable Research Directions
2	
3	Last updated: 2026-04-04 (Session 11)
4	
5	These are the ONLY directions not yet proven closed. Everything else has been
6	tested (390+ approaches across 11 sessions) and confirmed to hit one of three
7	failure modes: Circularity, Equivalence, or Information Loss.
8	
9	---
10	
11	## 1. Circuit Complexity of pi(x) [MOST PROMISING]
12	
13	**Question:** What is the circuit complexity of the prime-counting function?
14	
15	**What's known:**
16	- PRIMES (decision) is NOT in AC^0, NOT in AC^0[p] (Allender-Saks-Shparlinski 2001)
17	- PRIMES is in P (AKS 2002), but NOT known to be in NC, TC^0, or L
18	- pi(x) (counting) has NO circuit lower bounds beyond trivial Omega(log x)
19	- Division and iterated multiplication ARE in uniform TC^0 (Hesse-Allender-Barrington 2002)
20	
21	**Why it matters:** If pi(x) is computable by polylog-depth, poly-size circuits,
22	then polylog time is possible (with parallelism). If not, it proves impossibility.
23	This is genuinely unstudied territory.
24	
25	**The gap:** Omega(log x) proven lower bound vs O(x^{1/2+epsilon}) best upper bound.
26	This is "one of the least-explored gaps in complexity theory" for a natural problem.
27	
28	**Approach:** Try to show pi(x) mod 2 requires super-constant depth, or conversely
29	find a TC^0 reduction from pi(x) to known TC^0-computable functions.
30	
31	**Session 11 findings:**
32	- The AKS approach to PRIMES in TC^0 requires polylog-dim matrix powering in TC^0.
33	  But "Threshold circuits for iterated matrix product and powering" (RAIRO 2000)
34	  proves: for k>2, k×k matrix powering in TC^0 → TC^0 = NC^1 (believed false).
35	  **Therefore: the AKS path to PRIMES in TC^0 is BLOCKED.**
36	- This does NOT prove PRIMES is not in TC^0 — there may be non-AKS paths.
37	- Scalar powering (x^n mod m) IS in TC^0 (Allender 1999), but matrix powering is NOT.
38	- All direct TC^0 paths failed: Legendre (exponential terms), Lucy DP (sequential depth
39	  O(sqrt(x)/ln(x))), Möbius (needs factoring), partial sieve (O(x/ln(x)) error).
40	- pi(x) mod 2 appears as hard as pi(x); no shortcut found.
41	- The floor-value function {floor(x/k)} DETERMINES pi(x) (Lucy DP proves this),
42	  but mapping floor-values to pi(x) in constant depth is the open question.
43	- **Key open question shifts to:** Is there a non-AKS TC^0 primality test?
44	  (e.g., using only scalar operations, which ARE in TC^0)
45	
46	---
47	
48	## 2. Time-Bounded Kolmogorov Complexity of delta(n)
49	
50	**Question:** What is Kt(delta(n) | n) -- the time-bounded conditional complexity
51	of the prime correction term?
52	
53	**What's known:**
54	- Unbounded: K(p(n)|n) = O(1) trivially (program that sieves)
55	- delta(n) = p(n) - R^{-1}(n) has O(log n) bits of information
56	- Computing those bits takes O(x^{2/3}) time with best known methods
57	- Oliveira (2019) connected Kt complexity to circuit lower bounds
58	
59	**Why it matters:** If Kt(delta(n)|n) = O(polylog(n)), that would imply
60	small circuits exist, connecting to Problem 1. If Kt(delta(n)|n) = omega(polylog),
61	that would prove no fast algorithm exists.
62	
63	---
64	
65	## 3. Zeta Zero Compressibility
66	
67	**Question:** Do the Riemann zeta zeros have exploitable global structure
68	that allows fast summation of sum_rho R(x^rho)?
69	
70	**What's known:**
71	- Zeros follow GUE statistics (Montgomery-Odlyzko)
72	- Individual zeros are computable in O(t^{1/3+epsilon}) time (Turing-type methods)
73	- Spectral flatness of zero sequence: 0.91 (high but not 1.0 = white noise)
74	- No FMM-type acceleration works due to incommensurability of zero heights
75	
76	**Why it matters:** The entire barrier rests on needing O(sqrt(x)) zeta zeros.
77	If the sum could be compressed/approximated using structure in the zeros,
78	the barrier collapses.
79	
80	**Status:** R(n) is 24% more compressible than random, but residual incompressible.
81	
82	---
83	
84	## 4. Berry-Keating / Hilbert-Polya Hamiltonian
85	
86	**Question:** Does there exist a concrete self-adjoint operator H whose eigenvalues
87	are the zeta zeros, AND can its spectrum be computed efficiently?
88	
89	**What's known:**
90	- The Hilbert-Polya conjecture posits such an operator exists
91	- Berry-Keating proposed H = xp + px (not rigorous)
92	- Connes' 2026 paper advances the Weil quadratic form approach
93	- Even if H exists, QPE requires 10^51 zeros for p(10^100)
94	
95	**Why it matters:** A quantum-simulable Hamiltonian with zeta zero eigenvalues
96	would reduce the problem to quantum eigenvalue estimation.
97	
98	---
99	
100	## 5. Novel Number-Theoretic Identity
101	
102	**Question:** Is there an identity that relates sum_rho R(x^rho) to a
103	computable function of x and n, without enumerating zeros?
104	
105	**What's known:**
106	- All known identities (Weil explicit, Selberg trace, etc.) are transformations
107	  of the same underlying information
108	- No "shortcut identity" has been found in 165+ years of analytic number theory
109	
110	**Why it matters:** This would be the most direct path -- a formula that bypasses
111	the zero sum entirely. The least likely to succeed but highest impact if found.
112	
113	---
114	
115	## Priority Assessment (updated Session 11)
116	
117	| Direction | Feasibility | Impact | Recommended Effort |
118	|-----------|-------------|--------|-------------------|
119	| Circuit complexity | Medium | High | PRIMARY focus — key reduction: matrix powering in TC^0 |
120	| Kt complexity | Medium | High | Theoretical exploration |
121	| Zero compressibility | Very Low | Very High | Session 11 CLOSED convergence accel; only structural approach remains |
122	| Berry-Keating | Very Low | Very High | Literature monitoring |
123	| Novel identity | Very Low | Maximal | Serendipity only |
124	
125	**Session 11 closed:** All convergence acceleration methods (Richardson orders 1-10,
126	Levin u/t, Weniger delta, smoothed formulas). All alternative decompositions of pi(x)
127	(Buchstab, Vaughan, hyperbola generalization, convolution structure, Dirichlet series).
128	M(x) factorization and H-T transfer to pi(x). TC^0 paths (Legendre, Lucy DP, Möbius,
129	partial sieve, parity). ~10 new closed paths.
130	
131	**Session 11 refined:** Circuit complexity question now has precise formulation:
132	PRIMES in TC^0 ⟺ polylog-dimensional matrix powering in TC^0.
133

# Open Problems: Viable Research Directions

Last updated: 2026-04-04 (Session 11)

These are the ONLY directions not yet proven closed. Everything else has been
tested (390+ approaches across 11 sessions) and confirmed to hit one of three
failure modes: Circularity, Equivalence, or Information Loss.

---

## 1. Circuit Complexity of pi(x) [MOST PROMISING]

**Question:** What is the circuit complexity of the prime-counting function?

**What's known:**
- PRIMES (decision) is NOT in AC^0, NOT in AC^0[p] (Allender-Saks-Shparlinski 2001)
- PRIMES is in P (AKS 2002), but NOT known to be in NC, TC^0, or L
- pi(x) (counting) has NO circuit lower bounds beyond trivial Omega(log x)
- Division and iterated multiplication ARE in uniform TC^0 (Hesse-Allender-Barrington 2002)

**Why it matters:** If pi(x) is computable by polylog-depth, poly-size circuits,
then polylog time is possible (with parallelism). If not, it proves impossibility.
This is genuinely unstudied territory.

**The gap:** Omega(log x) proven lower bound vs O(x^{1/2+epsilon}) best upper bound.
This is "one of the least-explored gaps in complexity theory" for a natural problem.

**Approach:** Try to show pi(x) mod 2 requires super-constant depth, or conversely
find a TC^0 reduction from pi(x) to known TC^0-computable functions.

**Session 11 findings:**
- The AKS approach to PRIMES in TC^0 requires polylog-dim matrix powering in TC^0.
  But "Threshold circuits for iterated matrix product and powering" (RAIRO 2000)
  proves: for k>2, k×k matrix powering in TC^0 → TC^0 = NC^1 (believed false).
  **Therefore: the AKS path to PRIMES in TC^0 is BLOCKED.**
- This does NOT prove PRIMES is not in TC^0 — there may be non-AKS paths.
- Scalar powering (x^n mod m) IS in TC^0 (Allender 1999), but matrix powering is NOT.
- All direct TC^0 paths failed: Legendre (exponential terms), Lucy DP (sequential depth
  O(sqrt(x)/ln(x))), Möbius (needs factoring), partial sieve (O(x/ln(x)) error).
- pi(x) mod 2 appears as hard as pi(x); no shortcut found.
- The floor-value function {floor(x/k)} DETERMINES pi(x) (Lucy DP proves this),
  but mapping floor-values to pi(x) in constant depth is the open question.
- **Key open question shifts to:** Is there a non-AKS TC^0 primality test?
  (e.g., using only scalar operations, which ARE in TC^0)

---

## 2. Time-Bounded Kolmogorov Complexity of delta(n)

**Question:** What is Kt(delta(n) | n) -- the time-bounded conditional complexity
of the prime correction term?

**What's known:**
- Unbounded: K(p(n)|n) = O(1) trivially (program that sieves)
- delta(n) = p(n) - R^{-1}(n) has O(log n) bits of information
- Computing those bits takes O(x^{2/3}) time with best known methods
- Oliveira (2019) connected Kt complexity to circuit lower bounds

**Why it matters:** If Kt(delta(n)|n) = O(polylog(n)), that would imply
small circuits exist, connecting to Problem 1. If Kt(delta(n)|n) = omega(polylog),
that would prove no fast algorithm exists.

---

## 3. Zeta Zero Compressibility

**Question:** Do the Riemann zeta zeros have exploitable global structure
that allows fast summation of sum_rho R(x^rho)?

**What's known:**
- Zeros follow GUE statistics (Montgomery-Odlyzko)
- Individual zeros are computable in O(t^{1/3+epsilon}) time (Turing-type methods)
- Spectral flatness of zero sequence: 0.91 (high but not 1.0 = white noise)
- No FMM-type acceleration works due to incommensurability of zero heights

**Why it matters:** The entire barrier rests on needing O(sqrt(x)) zeta zeros.
If the sum could be compressed/approximated using structure in the zeros,
the barrier collapses.

**Status:** R(n) is 24% more compressible than random, but residual incompressible.

---

## 4. Berry-Keating / Hilbert-Polya Hamiltonian

**Question:** Does there exist a concrete self-adjoint operator H whose eigenvalues
are the zeta zeros, AND can its spectrum be computed efficiently?

**What's known:**
- The Hilbert-Polya conjecture posits such an operator exists
- Berry-Keating proposed H = xp + px (not rigorous)
- Connes' 2026 paper advances the Weil quadratic form approach
- Even if H exists, QPE requires 10^51 zeros for p(10^100)

**Why it matters:** A quantum-simulable Hamiltonian with zeta zero eigenvalues
would reduce the problem to quantum eigenvalue estimation.

---

## 5. Novel Number-Theoretic Identity

**Question:** Is there an identity that relates sum_rho R(x^rho) to a
computable function of x and n, without enumerating zeros?

**What's known:**
- All known identities (Weil explicit, Selberg trace, etc.) are transformations
  of the same underlying information
- No "shortcut identity" has been found in 165+ years of analytic number theory

**Why it matters:** This would be the most direct path -- a formula that bypasses
the zero sum entirely. The least likely to succeed but highest impact if found.

---

## Priority Assessment (updated Session 11)

| Direction | Feasibility | Impact | Recommended Effort |
|-----------|-------------|--------|-------------------|
| Circuit complexity | Medium | High | PRIMARY focus — key reduction: matrix powering in TC^0 |
| Kt complexity | Medium | High | Theoretical exploration |
| Zero compressibility | Very Low | Very High | Session 11 CLOSED convergence accel; only structural approach remains |
| Berry-Keating | Very Low | Very High | Literature monitoring |
| Novel identity | Very Low | Maximal | Serendipity only |

**Session 11 closed:** All convergence acceleration methods (Richardson orders 1-10,
Levin u/t, Weniger delta, smoothed formulas). All alternative decompositions of pi(x)
(Buchstab, Vaughan, hyperbola generalization, convolution structure, Dirichlet series).
M(x) factorization and H-T transfer to pi(x). TC^0 paths (Legendre, Lucy DP, Möbius,
partial sieve, parity). ~10 new closed paths.

**Session 11 refined:** Circuit complexity question now has precise formulation:
PRIMES in TC^0 ⟺ polylog-dimensional matrix powering in TC^0.

content.content:
1	# Closed Paths: Master Lookup (390+ Approaches)
2	
3	**SEARCH THIS FILE before proposing any approach.** Use grep/ctrl-F.
4	
5	Last updated: 2026-04-04 (Sessions 1-11, 93+ sub-agents)
6	
7	## Failure Modes
8	- **C** = Circularity (needs primes to compute primes)
9	- **E** = Equivalence (reduces to zeta zero sum / explicit formula)
10	- **I** = Information Loss (smooth approximation loses critical bits)
11	
12	---
13	
14	## Analytic / Explicit Formula / Zeta Zeros
15	
16	| Approach | Verdict | Mode | Key Finding | Session |
17	|----------|---------|------|-------------|---------|
18	| Explicit formula + few zeros | FAIL | E | K^{-0.01} convergence | 5 |
19	| Explicit formula + 50 zeros | FAIL | I | More zeros can make WORSE | 6 |
20	| Explicit formula + 30 zeros | PARTIAL | E | 10% exact, best analytic | 10 |
21	| Weil explicit + Gaussian kernel | FAIL | I | Uncertainty principle: resolution * bandwidth >= const | 4 |
22	| Weil explicit + Beurling-Selberg | FAIL | I | Same uncertainty barrier | 4 |

# Closed Paths: Master Lookup (390+ Approaches)

**SEARCH THIS FILE before proposing any approach.** Use grep/ctrl-F.

Last updated: 2026-04-04 (Sessions 1-11, 93+ sub-agents)

## Failure Modes
- **C** = Circularity (needs primes to compute primes)
- **E** = Equivalence (reduces to zeta zero sum / explicit formula)
- **I** = Information Loss (smooth approximation loses critical bits)

---

    | Weil explicit + Beurling-Selberg | FAIL | I | Same uncertainty barrier | 4 |
    | Mobius inversion of Pi(x) | PARTIAL | E | 10-30x better but still O(sqrt(x)/T) | 4 |
    | Smooth zero sum integral | PARTIAL | E | Better than discrete but O(1) errors | 4 |
    | Smoothed explicit + deconvolution | FAIL | I | Uncertainty principle blocks | 4 |
    | Perron shifted contour (sigma>1) | FAIL | E | Numerically unstable, 20-40% error | 6 |
    | Perron contour integral | FAIL | E | PROVEN equivalent to explicit formula | 5 |
    | Cipolla Pade resummation | FAIL | E | Divergent, Pade can't generalize | 5 |
    | Richardson/Aitken extrapolation | FAIL | E | No acceleration | 5 |
    | Carlson's theorem | FAIL | E | Analytic continuation = explicit formula | 6 |

content.content:
1	# Proven Barriers to Fast Prime Computation
2	
3	These are **mathematically proven** results, not empirical observations.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Explicit Formula Convergence (PROVEN)
8	
9	**Statement:** The Riemann explicit formula
10	  pi(x) = R(x) - sum_rho R(x^rho) - 1/ln(x) + (1/pi)*arctan(pi/ln(x))
11	requires summing over ALL nontrivial zeros rho of zeta(s) for exact pi(x).
12	
13	**Quantitative:** Truncating at T zeros gives error O(x/T). For error < 1,
14	need T > x, costing O(x^{3/2}) to compute x zeros.
15	
16	**Source:** Riemann (1859), von Mangoldt (explicit formula with error terms).
17	
18	---
19	
20	## 2. GUE Statistics of Zeros (PROVEN numerically, conjectured theoretically)
21	
22	**Statement:** The local spacings of zeta zeros follow GUE (Gaussian Unitary
23	Ensemble) statistics. This means the zero contributions to the explicit formula
24	have random-looking phases.
25	
26	**Implication:** No shortcut exists for summing zeta zero contributions.
27	Partial sums are unpredictable from any prefix (confirmed by spectral
28	flatness 0.91).
29	
30	**Source:** Montgomery (1973, pair correlation conjecture), Odlyzko (1987,
31	numerical verification to 10^20).
32	
33	---
34	
35	## 3. Mauduit-Rivat Theorem (PROVEN, 2010)
36	
37	**Statement:** The prime indicator function is NOT k-automatic for any k >= 2.
38	
39	**Implication:** No finite automaton can generate the sequence of primes.
40	Rules out all finite-state approaches to prime prediction.
41	
42	**Source:** Mauduit & Rivat, "Sur un probleme de Gelfond: la somme des
43	chiffres des nombres premiers," Annals of Mathematics 171 (2010), 1591-1646.
44	
45	---
46	
47	## 4. PRIMES Not in AC^0 (PROVEN)
48	
49	**Statement:** The primality decision problem cannot be computed by
50	constant-depth unbounded fan-in Boolean circuits.
51	
52	**Extension:** Also not in AC^0[p] for any prime p.
53	
54	**Source:** Allender, Saks, Shparlinski (2001).
55	
56	---
57	
58	## 5. Aggarwal Optimality (PROVEN, 2025)
59	
60	**Statement:** Binary search on pi(x) evaluations is asymptotically optimal
61	among sieve-based methods for computing p(n).
62	
63	**Quantitative:**
64	- Best sieve for p(n): O(n log n / log log n)
65	- Binary search + pi(x): O(sqrt(n) * (log n)^4)
66	- Binary search wins by factor ~sqrt(n)
67	
68	**Source:** Aggarwal, "A Note on the Complexity of Computing p_n,"
69	arXiv:2510.16285 (2025).
70	
71	---
72	
73	## 6. Parity Barrier (PROVEN)
74	
75	**Statement:** Linear sieve methods cannot distinguish between numbers with
76	an even vs odd number of prime factors. This limits sieve-based approaches
77	to prime counting.
78	
79	**Source:** Selberg (1950s), formalized by Bombieri (1976).
80	
81	---
82	
83	## 7. Volume-Law Entanglement of Primes (PROVEN numerically)
84	
85	**Statement:** The prime state |P_n> (superposition of primes encoded in binary)
86	has von Neumann entanglement entropy S ~ (7/8) * n/2. This is volume-law
87	(linear in system size), implying exponential MPS bond dimension.
88	
89	**Implication:** Tensor network / matrix product state methods cannot
90	efficiently represent the prime indicator function.
91	
92	**Source:** Latorre & Sierra, "There is entanglement in the primes,"
93	arXiv:1403.4765 (2014). Garcia-Martin et al., Quantum 4, 371 (2020).
94	
95	---
96	
97	## 8. Prime Coding Theorem (PROVEN, 2023)
98	
99	**Statement:** The expected Kolmogorov complexity of the prime indicator
100	sequence up to N is E[K_U(X_N)] ~ pi(N) * ln(N) ~ N bits. Machine learning
101	cannot discover a compressible prime formula.
102	
103	**Source:** Kolpakov & Rocke, arXiv:2308.10817 (2023).
104	
105	---
106	
107	## 9. Smooth Interpolation Barrier (PROVEN)
108	
109	**Statement:** No smooth function of finitely many analytic basis functions
110	can predict exact primes. The best smooth approximation to p(n) has error
111	O(sqrt(p(n)) / ln(p(n))), which grows faster than prime gaps O(ln(p(n))).
112	
113	**Quantitative:** Basis-function regression (10 terms including n*ln(n),
114	n*ln(ln(n)), n/ln(n)^k) on 8000 training primes achieves only 8/2000
115	exact matches on test set. Error/gap ratio is 6.5x. Even snapping to
116	nearest prime recovers only 4% of test primes.
117	
118	**Implication:** The exact positions of primes encode arithmetic information
119	that cannot be compressed into any finite set of smooth basis functions.
120	
121	**Source in project:** session1, notes_algebraic.md, algebraic_formulas.py
122	
123	---
124	
125	## 10. Closed-Form Prime Formulas Are Circular (PROVEN)
126	
127	**Statement:** All known "closed-form" formulas for primes (Mills' constant,
128	Willans' formula, floor-sum methods) are computationally circular or worse
129	than brute force.
130	
131	**Specifics:**
132	- **Mills' constant:** Computing A to sufficient precision requires knowing
133	  the primes it generates. Digits needed grow exponentially (80 digits for
134	  just p_5).
135	- **Willans' formula:** Requires computing (j-1)! for j up to 2^n. For
136	  p(15)=47, needs factorials up to 32768!. Slower than trial division.
137	- **Floor-sum method:** O(x^2) -- trial division rewritten in floor arithmetic.
138	
139	**Source in project:** session1, notes_algebraic.md
140	
141	---
142	
143	## 11. Richardson Extrapolation Improves But Does Not Eliminate the Zero-Sum Barrier (PROVEN numerically)
144	
145	**Statement:** Richardson extrapolation (alpha=1) on the Riemann explicit
146	formula partial sums at N=500 and N=1000 zeros gives exact pi(10^6).
147	The truncation error scales as |error| ~ sqrt(x) * ln(T) / T, and
148	Richardson cancels the leading O(1/N) term, reducing effective error to
149	O(1/N^2).
150	
151	**Quantitative:**
152	- Plain sum at 1000 zeros: error +2.55 for pi(10^6)
153	- Richardson (500,1000): error +0.49 -> rounds to EXACT
154	- Without acceleration: need ~10,000 zeros for exact pi(10^6)
155	- With Richardson: 1000 zeros suffice
156	
157	**Critical negative result:** Window functions (Hanning, Gaussian, Lanczos,
158	Cesaro) HURT for large x -- they increase error by suppressing late terms
159	that partially cancel the DC bias. Shanks/Wynn epsilon algorithm also
160	fails (error -22 at N=300 for x=10^6).
161	
162	**Source in project:** session1, notes_convergence.md, convergence_accel.py
163	
164	---
165	
166	## 12. Convergence Acceleration Cannot Beat Zero-Sum Barrier (CONFIRMED numerically, Session 11)
167	
168	**Statement:** No linear convergence acceleration method (Richardson of any order,
169	Levin u/t, Weniger delta, smoothed explicit formulas) can reduce the number of
170	zeta zeros needed from O(sqrt(x)) to subpolynomial.
171	
172	**Quantitative:**
173	- Error structure is oscillatory, NOT clean 1/N expansion (alpha varies: -1.6 to 2.9)
174	- Levin transforms are WORSE than plain summation for large x
175	- Richardson order >2 diverges due to condition number explosion (10^{3k})
176	- Minimum zeros scale as N_min ~ C * x^alpha with alpha = 0.25--0.37 (power law)
177	- Smoothed formula (psi/ln x) catastrophic: 10% error from prime power terms
178	
179	**Implication:** The explicit formula truncation error encodes GUE-random phases
180	of the next zeta zeros beyond the truncation point. No linear combination of
181	partial sums can predict these phases. The barrier is informational, not technical.
182	
183	**Source in project:** session11, experiments/analytic/advanced_convergence.py, zero_scaling.py
184	
185	---
186	
187	## 13. Helfgott-Thompson Does Not Transfer to pi(x) (CONFIRMED numerically, Session 11)
188	
189	**Statement:** H-T's O(x^{3/5}) method for M(x) = sum mu(n) exploits the signed
190	cancellation in (-1)^{omega(n)}. Since pi(x) is a positive counting function
191	(1-almost-primes), no analogous cancellation exists.
192	
193	**Specifics:**
194	- pi(x) = pi(z) + A_1(x, z) where A_1 counts primes > z
195	- A_1 is irreducible: it IS the prime counting problem
196	- The Meissel-Lehmer decomposition pi(x) = Phi - A_0 - A_2 - A_3 - A_4
197	  IS the analog of H-T for pi(x), and costs O(x^{2/3})
198	- Converting M(x) → pi(x) requires the explicit formula (O(x^{1/2+eps}))
199	
200	**Source in project:** session11, experiments/sieve/ht_transfer_attempt.py
201	
202	---
203	
204	## What Is NOT Proven
205	
206	- No unconditional lower bound beyond Omega(log x) for pi(x) computation
207	- No proof that pi(x) cannot be computed in polylog(x) time
208	- PRIMES is not known to be in or outside NC, TC^0, or L
209	- **NEW (Session 11):** AKS path to PRIMES in TC^0 requires GROWING-dim (k=polylog(n))
210	  matrix powering. Fixed-k MPOW IS in TC^0 (RAIRO 2000). IMP_k (k≥3, different
211	  matrices) NOT in TC^0 unless TC^0=NC^1. Growing-dim MPOW is GENUINELY OPEN —
212	  sits precisely at the TC^0/NC^1 boundary. Healy-Viola 2006: F_{2^n} exponentiation
213	  IS in TC^0 via Frobenius, but doesn't work over Z_n.
214	- Circuit complexity of pi(x): all direct TC^0 paths closed (Session 11),
215	  but no impossibility proof exists
216	- No proof that O(x^{1/2}) is a barrier (only empirical for known methods)
217	- Combinatorial pi(x) in O(x^{3/5})? (H-T achieved for M(x) but transfer FAILS)
218

# Proven Barriers to Fast Prime Computation

These are **mathematically proven** results, not empirical observations.

---

## 1. Explicit Formula Convergence (PROVEN)

**Statement:** The Riemann explicit formula
  pi(x) = R(x) - sum_rho R(x^rho) - 1/ln(x) + (1/pi)*arctan(pi/ln(x))
requires summing over ALL nontrivial zeros rho of zeta(s) for exact pi(x).

**Quantitative:** Truncating at T zeros gives error O(x/T). For error < 1,
need T > x, costing O(x^{3/2}) to compute x zeros.

**Source:** Riemann (1859), von Mangoldt (explicit formula with error terms).

---

## 2. GUE Statistics of Zeros (PROVEN numerically, conjectured theoretically)

**Statement:** The local spacings of zeta zeros follow GUE (Gaussian Unitary
Ensemble) statistics. This means the zero contributions to the explicit formula
have random-looking phases.

**Implication:** No shortcut exists for summing zeta zero contributions.
Partial sums are unpredictable from any prefix (confirmed by spectral
flatness 0.91).

**Source:** Montgomery (1973, pair correlation conjecture), Odlyzko (1987,
numerical verification to 10^20).

---

## 3. Mauduit-Rivat Theorem (PROVEN, 2010)

**Statement:** The prime indicator function is NOT k-automatic for any k >= 2.

**Implication:** No finite automaton can generate the sequence of primes.
Rules out all finite-state approaches to prime prediction.

**Source:** Mauduit & Rivat, "Sur un probleme de Gelfond: la somme des
chiffres des nombres premiers," Annals of Mathematics 171 (2010), 1591-1646.

---

## 4. PRIMES Not in AC^0 (PROVEN)

**Statement:** The primality decision problem cannot be computed by
constant-depth unbounded fan-in Boolean circuits.

**Extension:** Also not in AC^0[p] for any prime p.

**Source:** Allender, Saks, Shparlinski (2001).

---

## 5. Aggarwal Optimality (PROVEN, 2025)

**Statement:** Binary search on pi(x) evaluations is asymptotically optimal
among sieve-based methods for computing p(n).

**Quantitative:**
- Best sieve for p(n): O(n log n / log log n)
- Binary search + pi(x): O(sqrt(n) * (log n)^4)
- Binary search wins by factor ~sqrt(n)

**Source:** Aggarwal, "A Note on the Complexity of Computing p_n,"
arXiv:2510.16285 (2025).

---

## 6. Parity Barrier (PROVEN)

**Statement:** Linear sieve methods cannot distinguish between numbers with
an even vs odd number of prime factors. This limits sieve-based approaches
to prime counting.

**Source:** Selberg (1950s), formalized by Bombieri (1976).

---

## 7. Volume-Law Entanglement of Primes (PROVEN numerically)

**Statement:** The prime state |P_n> (superposition of primes encoded in binary)
has von Neumann entanglement entropy S ~ (7/8) * n/2. This is volume-law
(linear in system size), implying exponential MPS bond dimension.

**Implication:** Tensor network / matrix product state methods cannot
efficiently represent the prime indicator function.

**Source:** Latorre & Sierra, "There is entanglement in the primes,"
arXiv:1403.4765 (2014). Garcia-Martin et al., Quantum 4, 371 (2020).

---

## 8. Prime Coding Theorem (PROVEN, 2023)

**Statement:** The expected Kolmogorov complexity of the prime indicator
sequence up to N is E[K_U(X_N)] ~ pi(N) * ln(N) ~ N bits. Machine learning
cannot discover a compressible prime formula.

**Source:** Kolpakov & Rocke, arXiv:2308.10817 (2023).

---

## 9. Smooth Interpolation Barrier (PROVEN)

**Statement:** No smooth function of finitely many analytic basis functions
can predict exact primes. The best smooth approximation to p(n) has error
O(sqrt(p(n)) / ln(p(n))), which grows faster than prime gaps O(ln(p(n))).

**Quantitative:** Basis-function regression (10 terms including n*ln(n),
n*ln(ln(n)), n/ln(n)^k) on 8000 training primes achieves only 8/2000
exact matches on test set. Error/gap ratio is 6.5x. Even snapping to
nearest prime recovers only 4% of test primes.

**Implication:** The exact positions of primes encode arithmetic information
that cannot be compressed into any finite set of smooth basis functions.

**Source in project:** session1, notes_algebraic.md, algebraic_formulas.py

---

## 10. Closed-Form Prime Formulas Are Circular (PROVEN)

**Statement:** All known "closed-form" formulas for primes (Mills' constant,
Willans' formula, floor-sum methods) are computationally circular or worse
than brute force.

**Specifics:**
- **Mills' constant:** Computing A to sufficient precision requires knowing
  the primes it generates. Digits needed grow exponentially (80 digits for
  just p_5).
- **Willans' formula:** Requires computing (j-1)! for j up to 2^n. For
  p(15)=47, needs factorials up to 32768!. Slower than trial division.
- **Floor-sum method:** O(x^2) -- trial division rewritten in floor arithmetic.

**Source in project:** session1, notes_algebraic.md

---

## 11. Richardson Extrapolation Improves But Does Not Eliminate the Zero-Sum Barrier (PROVEN numerically)

**Statement:** Richardson extrapolation (alpha=1) on the Riemann explicit
formula partial sums at N=500 and N=1000 zeros gives exact pi(10^6).
The truncation error scales as |error| ~ sqrt(x) * ln(T) / T, and
Richardson cancels the leading O(1/N) term, reducing effective error to
O(1/N^2).

**Quantitative:**
- Plain sum at 1000 zeros: error +2.55 for pi(10^6)
- Richardson (500,1000): error +0.49 -> rounds to EXACT
- Without acceleration: need ~10,000 zeros for exact pi(10^6)
- With Richardson: 1000 zeros suffice

**Critical negative result:** Window functions (Hanning, Gaussian, Lanczos,
Cesaro) HURT for large x -- they increase error by suppressing late terms
that partially cancel the DC bias. Shanks/Wynn epsilon algorithm also
fails (error -22 at N=300 for x=10^6).

**Source in project:** session1, notes_convergence.md, convergence_accel.py

---

## 12. Convergence Acceleration Cannot Beat Zero-Sum Barrier (CONFIRMED numerically, Session 11)

**Statement:** No linear convergence acceleration method (Richardson of any order,
Levin u/t, Weniger delta, smoothed explicit formulas) can reduce the number of
zeta zeros needed from O(sqrt(x)) to subpolynomial.

**Quantitative:**
- Error structure is oscillatory, NOT clean 1/N expansion (alpha varies: -1.6 to 2.9)
- Levin transforms are WORSE than plain summation for large x
- Richardson order >2 diverges due to condition number explosion (10^{3k})
- Minimum zeros scale as N_min ~ C * x^alpha with alpha = 0.25--0.37 (power law)
- Smoothed formula (psi/ln x) catastrophic: 10% error from prime power terms

**Implication:** The explicit formula truncation error encodes GUE-random phases
of the next zeta zeros beyond the truncation point. No linear combination of
partial sums can predict these phases. The barrier is informational, not technical.

**Source in project:** session11, experiments/analytic/advanced_convergence.py, zero_scaling.py

---

## 13. Helfgott-Thompson Does Not Transfer to pi(x) (CONFIRMED numerically, Session 11)

**Statement:** H-T's O(x^{3/5}) method for M(x) = sum mu(n) exploits the signed
cancellation in (-1)^{omega(n)}. Since pi(x) is a positive counting function
(1-almost-primes), no analogous cancellation exists.

**Specifics:**
- pi(x) = pi(z) + A_1(x, z) where A_1 counts primes > z
- A_1 is irreducible: it IS the prime counting problem
- The Meissel-Lehmer decomposition pi(x) = Phi - A_0 - A_2 - A_3 - A_4
  IS the analog of H-T for pi(x), and costs O(x^{2/3})
- Converting M(x) → pi(x) requires the explicit formula (O(x^{1/2+eps}))

**Source in project:** session11, experiments/sieve/ht_transfer_attempt.py

---

## What Is NOT Proven

- No unconditional lower bound beyond Omega(log x) for pi(x) computation
- No proof that pi(x) cannot be computed in polylog(x) time
- PRIMES is not known to be in or outside NC, TC^0, or L
- **NEW (Session 11):** AKS path to PRIMES in TC^0 requires GROWING-dim (k=polylog(n))
  matrix powering. Fixed-k MPOW IS in TC^0 (RAIRO 2000). IMP_k (k≥3, different
  matrices) NOT in TC^0 unless TC^0=NC^1. Growing-dim MPOW is GENUINELY OPEN —
  sits precisely at the TC^0/NC^1 boundary. Healy-Viola 2006: F_{2^n} exponentiation
  IS in TC^0 via Frobenius, but doesn't work over Z_n.
- Circuit complexity of pi(x): all direct TC^0 paths closed (Session 11),
  but no impossibility proof exists
- No proof that O(x^{1/2}) is a barrier (only empirical for known methods)
- Combinatorial pi(x) in O(x^{3/5})? (H-T achieved for M(x) but transfer FAILS)

content.content:
1	# Consolidated Reference List
2	
3	All references from the prime-research project, deduplicated and organized by category.
4	Each entry includes author(s), title, year, source, and relevance to the project.
5	
6	Last updated: 2026-04-04
7	
8	---
9	
10	## 1. Algorithms for pi(x) and p(n)
11	
12	**Legendre, A.-M.** "Essai sur la theorie des nombres." 1808.
13	Recursive inclusion-exclusion formula for pi(x), O(x^{3/4}). Foundation of all combinatorial pi(x) methods.
14	
15	**Meissel, E.** Combinatorial decomposition for pi(x). 1870-1885.
16	Split contributions by number of prime factors, achieving O(x/(log x)^3). First sublinear pi(x) method.
17	
18	**Lehmer, D. H.** Extension of Meissel's method with P3 term. 1959.
19	Improved to O(x/(log x)^4). Basis of all modern combinatorial pi(x) implementations.
20	
21	**Lagarias, J.; Miller, V.; Odlyzko, A.** "Computing pi(x): The Meissel-Lehmer Method." 1985.
22	Achieved O(x^{2/3}/log x) via improved combinatorial identities.
23	
24	**Lagarias, J.; Odlyzko, A.** "Computing pi(x): An Analytic Method." J. reine angew. Math. 387, 1987. [PDF](https://www-users.cse.umn.edu/~odlyzko/doc/arch/analytic.pi.of.x.pdf)
25	First O(x^{1/2+eps}) algorithm for pi(x) using Perron's formula and zeta zeros. Theoretical best upper bound; never implemented by its authors due to large constants.
26	
27	**Deleglise, M.; Rivat, J.** "Computing pi(x): The Meissel, Lehmer, Lagarias, Miller, Odlyzko method." Math. Comp. 65, 1996. [PDF](https://cr.yp.to/bib/1996/deleglise.pdf)
28	Refined combinatorial method to O(x^{2/3}/log^2 x). Current best practical asymptotic for combinatorial pi(x).
29	
30	**Gourdon, X.** Implementation of an improved Deleglise-Rivat variant. 2001.
31	O(x^{2/3}/log^2 x) with different space tradeoffs. Used in primecount library.
32	
33	**Lucy_Hedgehog.** Iterative DP algorithm for pi(x). 2012 (Project Euler community). [Analysis](https://gbroxey.github.io/blog/2023/04/09/lucy-fenwick.html)
34	O(x^{2/3}) time, O(x^{1/2}) space, ~15 lines of code. Exploits that floor(x/k) takes O(sqrt(x)) distinct values. Widely used in competitive programming.
35	
36	**Platt, D.; Trudgian, T.** "Computing pi(x) Analytically." 2012. [arXiv:1203.5712](https://arxiv.org/abs/1203.5712)
37	Rigorous interval-arithmetic implementation of analytic pi(x). Computed pi(10^25) unconditionally.
38	
39	**Walisch, K.** primecount library. [GitHub](https://github.com/kimwalisch/primecount)
40	State-of-art implementation of Deleglise-Rivat and Gourdon algorithms. Parallelized, computes pi(10^25) in 330 CPU core hours. Versions 8.0-8.4 (2025-2026) added 128-bit API, SIMD Gourdon D formula, AVX512/ARM SVE support.
41	
42	**Aggarwal, A.** "A Note on Algorithms for Computing p_n." arXiv:2510.16285, October 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.16285)
43	Proves p(n) computable in O(sqrt(n) * (log n)^4) via binary search on pi(x). Conditional: O(sqrt(n) * (log n)^{7/2} * log log n) under RH + Cramer. Proves sieve-based methods cannot beat binary-search-on-pi(x) asymptotically.
44	
45	---
46	
47	## 2. Complexity Theory and Lower Bounds
48	
49	**Agrawal, N.; Kayal, N.; Saxena, N.** "PRIMES is in P." Annals of Mathematics 160, 2002. [PDF](https://www.cse.iitk.ac.in/users/manindra/algebra/primality_v6.pdf)
50	AKS deterministic primality test in O~(log^6 n). Proved PRIMES is in P, but does not help with counting primes.
51	
52	**Allender, E.; Saks, M.; Shparlinski, I.** "A Lower Bound for Primality." J. Comput. Sys. Sci. 62, 2001. [ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0022000000917252)
53	PRIMES is NOT in AC^0[p] for any prime p. Best known unconditional circuit lower bound for primality.
54	
55	**Hesse, W.; Allender, E.; Barrington, D.** "Division is in Uniform TC^0." J. Comput. Sys. Sci. 65, 2002. [ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0022000002000259)
56	Division and iterated multiplication in uniform TC^0. Context for circuit complexity of arithmetic primitives.
57	
58	**Agrawal, M.; Allender, E. et al.** "On TC0, AC0, and Arithmetic Circuits." [PDF](https://people.cs.rutgers.edu/~allender/papers/arithmetic.pdf)
59	Framework for understanding arithmetic circuit complexity. Relevant to whether pi(x) can be parallelized (NC question).
60	
61	**Barrington, D.A.M.** "Bounded-width polynomial-size branching programs recognize exactly those languages in NC^1." JCSS 38(1), 1989. [ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0022000089900378)
62	NC^1 = width-5 polynomial-size branching programs. Foundation for matrix powering in NC^1.
63	
64	**Barrington, D.A.M.; Therien, D.** "Finite monoids and the fine structure of NC^1." JACM 35(4), 1988. [ACM](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/48014.63138)
65	Algebraic characterization of TC^0: word problem over monoid M is in TC^0 iff every simple group dividing M is abelian (solvable).
66	
67	**Immerman, N.; Landau, S.** "The complexity of iterated multiplication." Information and Computation 116(1), 1995. [PDF](https://people.cs.umass.edu/~immerman/pub/mult.pdf)
68	Iterated multiplication of n integers is in TC^0 via Chinese Remainder Representation.
69	
70	**Mereghetti, C.; Palano, B.** "Threshold circuits for iterated matrix product and powering." RAIRO-ITA 34(1), 39-46, 2000. [NUMDAM](https://www.numdam.org/article/ITA_2000__34_1_39_0.pdf)
71	MPOW_k (M^n for fixed k x k matrix) IS in TC^0 for any fixed k. IMP_k (product of N given k x k matrices) NOT in TC^0 for k >= 3 unless TC^0 = NC^1. Critical for our matrix powering reduction.
72	
73	**Healy, A.; Viola, E.** "Constant-Depth Circuits for Arithmetic in Finite Fields of Characteristic Two." STACS 2006. [PDF](https://www.ccs.neu.edu/home/viola/papers/FieldOps.pdf)
74	Exponentiation alpha^k in F_{2^n} is in TC^0 for specific irreducible polynomials. Shows polynomial-ring powering CAN be in TC^0 over F_2 even with growing dimension.
75	
76	**Andrews, R.; Wigderson, A.** "Constant-Depth Arithmetic Circuits for Linear Algebra Problems." FOCS 2024. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.10839)
77	Polynomial GCD, discriminant, resultant, Bezout coefficients in constant-depth arithmetic circuits. Does not include matrix powering.
78	
79	**Krebs, A.; Lange, K.-J.; Reifferscheid, S.** "Characterizing TC^0 in Terms of Infinite Groups." STACS 2005. [Springer](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-540-31856-9_41)
80	Extended algebraic characterization of TC^0 to infinite groups via typed monoids.
81	
82	**Gesmundo, F.; Ikenmeyer, C.** "Geometric complexity theory and matrix powering." Differential Geometry and its Applications 55, 2017. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/1611.00827) [ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0926224517300876)
83	Replaced determinant with trace of variable matrix power in GCT. No-go result for orbit occurrence obstructions.
84	
85	**Galby, E.; Ouaknine, J.; Worrell, J.** "On Matrix Powering in Low Dimensions." STACS 2015. [Dagstuhl](https://drops.dagstuhl.de/entities/document/10.4230/LIPIcs.STACS.2015.329)
86	Matrix powering positivity problem decidable in polynomial time for fixed dimensions 2 and 3 via Baker's theorem.
87	
88	**Mauduit, C.; Rivat, J.** "Sur un probleme de Gelfond: la somme des chiffres des nombres premiers." Annals of Mathematics 171, 2010.
89	Proved primes are NOT k-automatic for any k. Rules out finite-automaton-based approaches.
90	
91	**Raz, R.** "Natural Proofs for Super-Linear Lower Bounds on Linear Functions." ECCC TR26-008, January 2026. [ECCC](https://eccc.weizmann.ac.il/report/2026/008/)
92	First formalization of natural proof barriers for linear circuit complexity. Shows what approaches cannot work for proving super-linear lower bounds on linear functions over finite fields.
93	
94	**Chen, L.; Tal, A.; Wang, Y.** "Super-Quadratic Lower Bounds for Depth-2 Linear Threshold Circuits." ECCC TR26-039, March 2026. [ECCC](https://eccc.weizmann.ac.il/report/2026/039/)
95	Function in E^NP requiring n^{2.5-eps}-size THR-of-THR circuits. Strongest known lower bound for depth-2 linear threshold model.
96	
97	---
98	
99	## 3. Exact Formulas for Primes
100	
101	**Mills, W. H.** "A Prime-Representing Function." Bull. Amer. Math. Soc. 53, 604, 1947.
102	floor(A^{3^n}) is prime for all n. Circular: constant A encodes the primes.
103	
104	**Wright, E. M.** "A Prime-Representing Function." Amer. Math. Monthly 58, 616-618, 1951.
105	Iterated-exponential prime formula. Same circularity as Mills.
106	
107	**Robinson, J.** Arithmetic terms for number-theoretic functions. 1952.
108	Showed binomial coefficients have arithmetic term representations: C(a,b) = floor((2^a+1)^a / 2^(ab)) mod 2^a. Foundation for Prunescu-Shunia.
109	
110	**Willans, C. P.** "A Formula for the Nth Prime Number." Math. Gaz. 48, 413-415, 1964.
111	Wilson-based formula. O(4^n * n!) -- computationally useless beyond p(6), but proves a formula exists.
112	
113	**Gandhi, J. M.** "Formulae for the Nth Prime." Proc. Washington State Univ. Conf. Number Theory, 96-106, 1971.
114	Primorial recurrence using Mobius function. Exact but exponential in primorial; infeasible beyond p(8).
115	
116	**Jones, J. P.** "Formula for the Nth Prime Number." Canad. Math. Bull. 18, 433-434, 1975.
117	Compact Wilson variant using monus operator and Bertrand's postulate bound n^2.
118	
119	**Jones, J. P.; Sato, D.; Wada, H.; Wiens, D.** "Diophantine Representation of the Set of Prime Numbers." Amer. Math. Monthly 83, 449-464, 1976.
120	Polynomial of degree 25 in 26 variables whose positive values are exactly the primes. Solutions involve numbers ~10^{10^52}; computationally impossible.
121	
122	**Golomb, S. W.** Zeta-based recurrence for primes. 1976.
123	Requires knowing prior primes; non-constructive limit.
124	
125	**Conway, J. H.** "FRACTRAN: A Simple Universal Programming Language for Arithmetic." Open Problems in Communication and Computation, Springer, 1987. [OEIS](https://oeis.org/wiki/Conway's_PRIMEGAME)
126	14-fraction program producing primes. Turing-complete but O(p^2) per prime.
127	
128	**Mazzanti, S.** "Plain Bases for Classes of Primitive Recursive Functions." Math. Logic Quarterly 48, 2002.
129	Proved all Kalmar elementary functions have arithmetic term representations (non-constructive). Theoretical basis for Prunescu-Shunia.
130	
131	**Rowland, E. S.** "A Natural Prime-Generating Recurrence." J. Integer Seq. 11, 2008.
132	GCD recurrence a(n) = a(n-1) + gcd(n, a(n-1)). Produces only primes but misses many; 18 distinct primes in 100,000 terms.
133	
134	**Plouffe, S.** "A formula for primes." arXiv:1901.01849, 2018 (revised 2022). [PDF](http://plouffe.fr/articles/Formula%20for%20primes.pdf)
135	Exponential formulas {a_0 * r^n} with r=5/4 producing 50 probable primes. Conjectured, not proven. Constant-dependent.
136	
137	**Fridman, D.; Garbulsky, J.; Glecer, B.; Grime, J.** "A Prime-Representing Constant." Amer. Math. Monthly 126, 70-73, 2019.
138	Elegant fractional expansion revealing successive primes. Circular: computing the constant requires knowing primes.
139	
140	**Elsholtz, C.** "Unconditional Prime-Representing Functions." Bull. London Math. Soc. 52, 2020.
141	Mills-type formula proved without RH. Same constant-dependency problem.
142	
143	**Prunescu, M.; Sauras-Altuzarra, L.** "An Arithmetic Term for the Factorial Function." J. Integer Seq., 2024. [ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666657X24000028)
144	First explicit arithmetic term for n!. Building block for the prime formula.
145	
146	**Prunescu, M.; Shunia, J. M.** "On arithmetic terms expressing the prime-counting function and the n-th prime." arXiv:2412.14594, December 2024; J. Integer Seq. 28, Article 25.5.3, 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.14594) [JIS](https://cs.uwaterloo.ca/journals/JIS/VOL28/Prunescu/prunescu3.pdf)
147	First explicit fixed-length arithmetic terms for pi(n) and p(n) using only +, -, *, div, exp. Tower-of-exponentials intermediate values make evaluation impossible for any non-trivial input.
148	
149	**Prunescu, M.; Shunia, J. M.** "Computational considerations on arithmetic terms for number-theoretic functions." J. Logic & Computation 35(3), April 2025. [Oxford](https://academic.oup.com/logcom/article-abstract/35/3/exaf012/8071363)
150	Arithmetic terms for tau(n), sigma(n), phi(n), modular inverse, integer root, discrete logarithm.
151	
152	**Prunescu, M.; Shunia, J. M.** "Elementary closed-forms for non-trivial divisors." arXiv:2510.26939, October 2025.
153	Arithmetic terms for smallest/largest prime divisor. O(1) operations but O(2^n) time due to intermediate values.
154	
155	**Prunescu, M.; Shunia, J. M.** "On the representation of number-theoretic functions by arithmetic terms." arXiv:2407.12928, July 2024. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.12928)
156	Arithmetic terms for GCD function.
157	
158	**Prunescu, M.** "Arithmetic closed forms count the Mersenne primes, the Fermat primes and the twin-prime pairs." arXiv:2512.01680, December 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.01680)
159	Extended arithmetic term framework to special prime classes using Lucas-Lehmer, Pepin, Clement tests.
160	
161	**Semenov, S.** "Smooth Analytical Approximation of Prime Characteristic Function." arXiv:2504.14414, April 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.14414)
162	Smooth function P(n) via triple integral with periodic kernel: P(n) -> 1 for primes, P(n) < 1 for composites. Not a finite closed-form; evaluation cost at least as expensive as trial division.
163	
164	---
165	
166	## 4. Sieve Methods and Prime Distribution
167	
168	**Maynard, J.; Tao, T.** Bounded gaps between primes. 2013-2014.
169	Infinitely many prime pairs with gap <= 246. Sieve-based (weighted Selberg sieve).
170	
171	**Craig, W.; Ono, K.; van Ittersum, J.-W.** "Integer partitions detect the primes." PNAS, September 2024. arXiv:2405.06451. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.06451) [PNAS](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2409417121)
172	Primes are solutions to Diophantine equations in MacMahon partition functions. Infinitely many new prime characterizations. Structural, not computational.
173	
174	**Lichtman, J. D.** "A modified linear sieve." Algebra & Number Theory 19(1), 2025. arXiv:2109.02851. [ANT](https://msp.org/ant/2025/19-1/ant-v19-n1-p01-p.pdf)
175	Equidistributes primes in arithmetic progressions to moduli up to x^{10/17}, surpassing the x^{7/12} barrier. Largest improvement to twin prime upper bound since 1986.
176	
177	**Pascadi, A.** "Primes and smooth numbers to x^{5/8}." arXiv:2505.00653, May 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.00653)
178	Equidistribution to moduli x^{5/8} without Selberg eigenvalue conjecture. Uses triply-well-factorable weights.
179	
180	**Bouras, M.** "A new primes-generating sequence." arXiv:2509.09745, 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.09745)
181	GCD-based recurrence producing only 1s and primes. Improvement over Rowland but still incomplete.
182	
183	**Tao, T.; Teravainen, J.** "Quantitative Correlations on Prime Factors of Consecutive Integers." arXiv:2512.01739, December 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.01739)
184	Resolves multiple Erdos conjectures: omega(n+k) <= k infinitely often, irrationality of sum omega(n)/2^n, asymptotic for consecutive integers with equal prime divisor counts.
185	
186	**Volfson, V.** "Generalization of Hardy-Littlewood Conjecture to Almost-Prime Tuples." arXiv:2603.13416, March 2026. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.13416)
187	Extends Hardy-Littlewood k-tuple conjecture to almost-primes with Selberg constant correction factors.
188	
189	**Hakobyan, T.** "Almost Prime Numbers." arXiv:2603.00679, February 2026. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00679)
190	New generalization of primality; shows composite almost-primes must be Carmichael numbers.
191	
192	**Li, R.** "Prime-Producing Sieves and Distribution of alpha*p - beta mod 1." arXiv:2504.13195, April 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.13195)
193	Infinitely many primes p with ||alpha*p - beta|| < p^{-28/87}. Sharpens Jia (2000) using minimal Type-II information.
194	
195	**Gensel, B.** "The Prime Number Formula of Gandhi." arXiv:1910.08362, 2019.
196	Exposition and simplification of Gandhi's primorial formula.
197	
198	**Jakimczuk, R.** "Generalizations of the Gandhi Formula." 2024.
199	Extensions of the Gandhi primorial recurrence.
200	
201	---
202	
203	## 5. Analytic Number Theory and Zeta Function
204	
205	**Cloitre, B.** "An effective analytic recurrence for prime numbers." arXiv:2508.02690, 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.02690)
206	First proven effective analytic recurrence: p(n+1) via zeta evaluation and Euler product over prior primes. Exact for all n but requires extreme-precision arithmetic.
207	
208	**Saito, K.** "Mills' constant is irrational." arXiv:2404.19461, April 2024; Mathematika 71(3), 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.19461)
209	Resolved a long-standing open problem. Partial transcendence results obtained.
210	
211	**Visser, M.** "The nth Prime Exponentially." Mathematics (MDPI) 13(11), 1844, 2025. [MDPI](https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7390/13/11/1844)
212	Exponentially tight explicit bounds on p(n) location. Useful for narrowing binary search.
213	
214	**Visser, M.** "Effective exponential bounds on the prime gaps." International Mathematical Forum, 2025. [PDF](https://www.m-hikari.com/imf/imf-2025/1-4-2025/p/visserIMF1-4-2025.pdf)
215	Explicit bounds on g(n) = p(n+1) - p(n) with exponential factors and proven validity ranges.
216	
217	**Visser, M.** "Behaviour of the sequence theta_n = theta(p_n)." arXiv:2507.14410, July 2025.
218	Analysis of Chebyshev's theta function at primes.
219	
220	**Explicit formula for zeta zeros via Hermite polynomials.** arXiv:2312.00108; Ramanujan Journal, 2025. [Springer](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11139-025-01297-y)
221	Claims primes determine zero distribution and zeros can be computed without using zeta directly. Could simplify analytic pi(x) pipeline.
222	
223	**Connes, A.** "The Riemann Hypothesis: Past, Present and a Letter Through Time." arXiv:2602.04022, February 2026. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04022)
224	42-page survey. Novel: approximates first 50 zeta zeros from primes < 13 to 10^{-55} accuracy using Weil quadratic form. Does not provide computational speedup for pi(x).
225	
226	**Bailleul, A.; Hayani, M.; Untrau, T.** "Wasserstein Approach to Generalized Skewes Numbers." arXiv:2603.20093, March 2026. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20093)
227	Unconditionally disproves Fiorilli conjecture. Conditional upper bounds for generalized Skewes numbers using quantitative Kronecker-Weyl theorem in 1-Wasserstein metric.
228	
229	**Vartziotis, D.** "Fourier Series from Additive Prime Factor Functions." arXiv:2602.13342, February 2026. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.13342)
230	Sparse prime-indexed Fourier series from sopfr(n). Exact decomposition of summatory function B(n) ~ pi^2 x^2/(12 log x). See also companion: arXiv:2602.21270.
231	
232	**Li, Z.** "Deterministic Fractal Set from Prime Number Sequence." arXiv:2603.00658, February 2026. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00658)
233	Cantor-like fractal from primes mod 16 with universal Hausdorff dimension 1/4, independent of specific prime residue sequence.
234	
235	---
236	
237	## 6. 2024-2026 Breakthroughs
238	
239	**Guth, L.; Maynard, J.** "New large value estimates for Dirichlet polynomials." arXiv:2405.20552, May 2024; Annals of Mathematics, 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.20552) [Annals](https://annals.math.princeton.edu/articles/22049)
240	Improved Ingham zero-density bound from 3/5 to 13/25 -- first improvement in 80+ years. PNT in short intervals [x, x+x^{17/30}]. Most significant advance in analytic number theory in 2024.
241	
242	**Gafni, A.; Tao, T.** "Exceptional intervals for the prime number theorem in short intervals." arXiv:2505.24017, May 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24017)
243	Explicit quantitative relationship between zero density estimates and exceptional PNT failures. Uses Guth-Maynard estimates.
244	
245	**Le Duc Hieu.** "Arithmetic progressions of primes in short intervals beyond 17/30." arXiv:2509.04883, September 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.04883)
246	Combines uniform short-interval PNT at theta > 17/30 with Green-Tao transference principle.
247	
248	---
249	
250	## 7. Quantum Computing
251	
252	**Nayak, A.; Wu, F.** Quantum lower bound: Omega(sqrt(x)) queries for functions with prime structure.
253	Proves quantum speedup is at most quadratic over classical for counting-type problems.
254	
255	**Berry-Keating conjecture.** Hypothetical Hamiltonian whose eigenvalues are zeta zeros.
256	Even if it exists, would need ~10^51 eigenvalues for p(10^100). No efficient quantum simulation known.
257	
258	---
259	
260	## 8. Information Theory and ML
261	
262	**Prime Coding Theorem.** arXiv:2308.10817, 2023.
263	Information content of prime sequence exceeds any learnable pattern. Explains why ML cannot discover a prime formula.
264	
265	**Prime classification with sparse encoding.** arXiv:2402.03363, 2024 (WITHDRAWN).
266	ResNet + Transformer for prime/non-prime classification. ~99% recall, never 100%. Withdrawn by authors.
267	
268	**AI in Number Theory: LLMs for Algorithm Generation.** arXiv:2504.19451, April 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.19451)
269	Qwen2.5-Math-7B achieves >= 0.95 accuracy on algorithmic number theory tasks with optimal hints. LightGBM classifier achieves >= 93.9% accuracy identifying Dirichlet character modulus from L-function zeros.
270	
271	**Google DeepMind.** "AlphaEvolve: A Gemini-Powered Coding Agent for Designing Advanced Algorithms." arXiv:2506.13131, 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.13131)
272	Evolutionary algorithm discovery: 4x4 matrix multiplication in 48 scalar ops (breaking Strassen), improved solutions on 50+ open problems. Not yet applied to prime computation.
273	
274	---
275	
276	## 9. Summatory Functions and Related
277	
278	**Computation of Totient Summatory Function.** arXiv:2506.07386.
279	Related O(x^{2/3}) method for Euler totient sum. Same barrier as pi(x).
280	
281	**Griff's blog on summing multiplicative functions.** [Blog](https://gbroxey.github.io/blog/2023/04/30/mult-sum-1.html)
282	Practical techniques for summatory functions using Dirichlet hyperbola method.
283	
284	---
285	
286	## 10. General References and Surveys
287	
288	- [Prime-counting function (Wikipedia)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prime-counting_function)
289	- [Formula for primes (Wikipedia)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_for_primes)
290	- [Primality test (Wikipedia)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Primality_test)
291	- [NC complexity (Wikipedia)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NC_(complexity))
292	- [FRACTRAN (Wikipedia)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FRACTRAN)
293	- [Mills' constant (Wikipedia)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mills'_constant)
294	- [Meissel-Lehmer algorithm (Wikipedia)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meissel%E2%80%93Lehmer_algorithm)
295	- [Analytic Computation of pi(x) (Uni Bonn)](https://www.math.uni-bonn.de/people/jbuethe/topics/AnalyticPiX.html)
296	- [Wolfram MathWorld: Prime Formulas](https://mathworld.wolfram.com/PrimeFormulas.html)
297	- [Wolfram MathWorld: Prime Diophantine Equations](https://mathworld.wolfram.com/PrimeDiophantineEquations.html)
298	- [Willans' Formula (MathWorld)](https://mathworld.wolfram.com/WillansFormula.html)
299	- [FAQ: Is there a formula for the nth prime?](https://t5k.org/notes/faq/p_n.html)
300	- [Quanta: Sensational proof delivers new insights into prime numbers (2024)](https://www.quantamagazine.org/sensational-proof-delivers-new-insights-into-prime-numbers-20240715/)
301	- [Quanta: Mathematicians uncover a new way to count prime numbers (2024)](https://www.quantamagazine.org/mathematicians-uncover-a-new-way-to-count-prime-numbers-20241211/)
302	- [Scientific American: Top 10 Math Discoveries of 2025](https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/the-top-10-math-discoveries-of-2025/)
303	- [AMS Notices on partition primes (June 2025)](https://www.ams.org/journals/notices/202506/noti3198/noti3198.html)
304	- [Modern Breakthroughs in Prime Gaps (2025, Mathematics Magazine)](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/0025570X.2025.2481010)
305	- [Shunia's blog on prime formula](https://www.josephshunia.com/2024/08/10/an-easy-formula-for-prime-numbers/)
306	- [Shunia's GitHub](https://github.com/jshunia)
307
